params.text:
The duty to make reasonable adjustments

Under the Equality Act 2010, disability is a protected characteristic, and organisations have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments so that disabled people are not put at a disadvantage. This applies across health and social care, and it is anticipatory: services should plan ahead rather than wait for a problem.

A reasonable adjustment is a change that removes or reduces a barrier. It does not mean giving someone an unfair advantage — it means giving them fair access to the same care and opportunities as everyone else.

  • Legal, not optional: failing to adjust can be unlawful discrimination.
  • Individual: what is reasonable depends on the person and the situation.
  • Everyday: many adjustments are small, cost little, and take moments.

Adjustments in practice

Reasonable adjustments are practical and personal. The key is to ask the individual what helps them, and then to make it happen.

  • Communication: easy-read information, extra time, pictures, or a communication aid.
  • Environment: a quieter room, dimmed lights, or the first or last appointment of the day.
  • Time: longer appointments so that nothing is rushed.
  • Familiarity: the same worker where possible, and a trusted person present.
  • Preparation: a visit in advance so a place or procedure feels less frightening.

Recording someone's adjustments clearly, so every worker knows them, means the person does not have to explain their needs over and over. Consistency is part of respect.

params.question: Making reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010 is a legal duty, not just a nice thing to do when there is time.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Reasonable adjustments are a legal duty under the Equality Act 2010, not an optional extra.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The duty to make reasonable adjustments is a legal requirement, and failing to make them can amount to unlawful discrimination.
